chief_complaint: A 37-year-old man presented with recurrent chest pain and suspected acute coronary syndrome.
history: The patient had a background of dyslipidemia, heavy smoking, and illicit drug use. He presented with recurrent chest pain, and electrocardiography revealed anteroseptal ST-segment elevation. Initial troponin was negative.
exam: Vital signs and physical examination findings are not explicitly stated.
labs: Electrocardiography: anteroseptal ST-segment elevation. Troponin: negative. Coronary angiography: anomalous LAD originating from the RCA with myocardial bridging at the mid-segment. Computed tomography coronary angiography: malignant inter-arterial course. Submaximal stress SPECT perfusion study: 77% of target heart rate, reproducing chest pain and revealing a small, reversible perfusion defect in the anteroseptal wall.
correct_diagnosis: Myocardial ischemia secondary to anomalous origin of left anterior descending coronary artery
differentials: Myocardial ischemia secondary to anomalous origin of left anterior descending coronary artery
correct_ranking: Myocardial ischemia secondary to anomalous origin of left anterior descending coronary artery
